perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Customer" means a person or entity that has acquired, or has indicated its interest in acquiring from Licensee, or from a Redistributor if so specified herein, a non-exclusive and nontransferable Sublicense to use one (1) or more of the Licensed Products.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Customer" means a person or entity that has acquired, or has indicated its interest in acquiring from Licensee, or from a Redistributor if so specified herein, an exclusive and transferable Sublicense to use one (1) or more of the Licensed Products.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition states that the sublicense is "non-exclusive and non-transferable". By changing this to "exclusive and transferable" we are altering the fundamental nature of the license granted to the customer. Making a license exclusive can dramatically change how the law views its protection and enforcement. Allowing transfer can also bring in complexity with respect to further obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts copyright law, which dictates that the terms of a transfer determine the scope of rights granted. By making the sublicense 'exclusive and transferable' without explicit consent from the copyright holder (Licensor), Licensee may be exceeding its authorized rights under the Copyright Act, potentially infringing on the Licensor's copyright.

Ownership of copyright (a) Initial Ownership .-Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire .-In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by th
perturbation.justification: The original contract defines a sublicense as "non-exclusive and non-transferable." The changed text modifies this to "exclusive and transferable." This alteration directly impacts the licensor's rights, potentially infringing on their copyright if the license doesn't explicitly allow for exclusive and transferable sublicenses. The Law Explanation points out this potential infringement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 USC 201, addresses "Ownership of copyright" and states that copyright vests initially in the author. By granting an exclusive and transferable sublicense without the copyright holder's explicit consent (as implied by the Law Explanation), the Licensee may be exceeding its rights, contradicting the copyright ownership rights. The change fundamentally alters the nature of the sublicense, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.4 "Licensed Product" means any copy, or part thereof, of object code of the software products listed on EXHIBIT A attached to this Agreement, as well as any Upgrades or other material distributed to Licensee by Licensor in connection with such software products.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 "Licensed Product" means any copy, or part thereof, of source code of the software products listed on EXHIBIT A attached to this Agreement, as well as any Upgrades or other material distributed to Licensee by Licensor in connection with such software products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "object code" to "source code" fundamentally alters the nature of the licensed material. Object code is the compiled, machine-readable form of software, while source code is the human-readable form. Granting access to source code grants greater modification abilities and requires more protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: Disclosing source code without proper safeguards can jeopardize its trade secret status. The law defines trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. Providing source code significantly increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or use, potentially nullifying its protection as a trade secret if the source code isn't adequately secured or if reverse engineering protections are circumvented.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The change from "object code" to "source code" creates a legal contradiction because it drastically alters the level of access and control the licensee has over the software, thereby increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation. The LLM Law Explanation highlights that disclosing source code without safeguards can jeopardize trade secret status. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1839, defines terms related to the protection of trade secrets, establishing a legal framework for their safeguarding, and the change weakens that protection. While the snippet focuses on defining terms like "foreign instrumentality" and "foreign agent", its very presence underscores the legal importance of trade secret protection, which is undermined by the increased exposure that providing source code entails.

perturbation.original_text: Section 7.3 EXPORTING AND SHIPMENT. Licensee shall obtain prior written approval from Licensor and any required export licenses from the United States Department of Commerce, Office of Export Administration or other applicable domestic or foreign governmental agency before exporting any Licensed Product or Documentation from the United States.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 7.3 EXPORTING AND SHIPMENT. Licensee shall obtain prior written approval from Licensor, but is not required to obtain any export licenses from the United States Department of Commerce, Office of Export Administration or other applicable domestic or foreign governmental agency before exporting any Licensed Product or Documentation from the United States.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the licensee's explicit obligation to obtain export licenses. Export licenses are often legally required based on the type of software, the destination country, and the intended end-user.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR governs the export and re-export of goods, software, and technology from the United States. Certain software and technical data require export licenses depending on factors like the destination country and the end-user. By stating that the Licensee is not required to obtain export licenses, the contract directly contradicts the EAR, potentially leading to violations of U.S. export control laws.

Enhanced Content - Developer Tools eCFR Content The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) is the official legal print publication containing the codification of the general and permanent rules published in the Federal Register by the departments and agencies of the Federal Government. The Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (eCFR) is a continuously updated online version of the CFR. It is not an official legal edition of the CFR. Learn more about the eCFR, its status, and the editorial process. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the licensee's obligation to obtain export licenses, which contradicts U.S. export control laws as explained in the Law Explanation. The EAR, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, governs the export of software and technology and often requires export licenses. By stating the licensee is 'not required' to obtain these licenses, the contract directly opposes the EAR's requirements, potentially leading to violations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the 'Export Administration Regulations' under 15 CFR Chapter VII Subchapter C, further solidifying the legal basis for export license requirements.
